career_path.role: Regional Scout → National Scout → Assistant Director of College Scouting → Director of College Scouting
VP of Player Personnel → Assistant General Manager

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: John Lynch / Kyle Shanahan (49ers system). Peters was Lynch's first hire and right-hand man for 7 years.
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Belichick tree (via Pioli/Caserio) → Elway/Broncos tree → Lynch/Shanahan 49ers tree. The Belichick influence shows in the collaborative, process-driven scouting approach and emphasis on versatility. The 49ers influence is the dominant signal: scheme-fit emphasis, valuing versatile athletes who can be developed, extracting value from middle rounds (George Kittle in Rd 5, Brock Purdy in Rd 7, Fred Warner, Dre Greenlaw), using analytics/R&D alongside traditional scouting, and trusting the board over need.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Peters appears more willing to trade away future picks for veteran acquisitions than the Lynch/Shanahan model typically was (Tunsil trade, Lattimore trade, Samuel trade). The 49ers were known for letting expensive free agents walk for comp picks; Peters has been more aggressive acquiring players via trade. This is a meaningful departure from the 49ers tree, though it may reflect the urgency of a rebuild rather than a philosophical shift.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: 2 drafts of data with Washington. The 49ers tree remains highly predictive for his evaluation philosophy (board-driven BPA, collaborative process, athlete-over-production at certain positions). Lineage weight will drop to 0.30 after 2026 draft. Trade behavior appears to be his own evolution.

key_influencers.name: Dan Quinn
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Defensive-minded, Seattle/Pete Carroll tree. Known for Cover-3/Cover-1 man-heavy scheme, though 2026 scheme shifting to new DC Daronte Jones' multiple system. Quinn values physical, long cornerbacks, versatile defensive linemen, and players who can play multiple roles. Has input on all defensive draft picks.

key_influencers.name: Lance Newmark
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Came with Peters from the 49ers. Deep alignment with Peters' philosophy. Handles day-to-day personnel and speaks at press conferences alongside Peters.

key_influencers.name: David Blackburn
key_influencers.title: Director of Player Personnel
key_influencers.known_biases: Formerly director of college scouting with the Baltimore Ravens. Brings Ravens-style evaluation emphasis on physical, high-character players.

key_influencers.name: Josh Harris
key_influencers.title: Managing Partner/Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: Harris hired advisors Bob Myers (former Warriors GM) and Rick Spielman (former Vikings GM) to assist with the GM/HC search. Harris is hands-on in culture and business decisions but has largely deferred football decisions to Peters. Magic Johnson is part of ownership group and was involved in GM interview process.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative front office. Peters runs a highly collaborative process where scouts, coaches, and the R&D/analytics department all contribute to building the draft board. Peters has final say. Quinn is consulted on picks as they happen, particularly on the defensive side. The coaching staff's scheme requirements directly influence the board — players who don't fit the scheme may not appear on Washington's board at all, regardless of consensus ranking.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Build through the draft. Take the best player on their board. Fill needs through free agency to create optionality in the draft. Peters stated 'I don't think we have a philosophy of going for premium positions in any round.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Strongly BPA-driven but filtered through a scheme-fit lens. Peters' board is built collaboratively with coaches, so 'BPA' means 'best player available who fits what the coaching staff wants to do.' In 2024, he took DT Jer'Zhan Newton at 36 despite not needing a DT (already had Jonathan Allen and Daron Payne), clearly a BPA pick. In 2025, he took OT Josh Conerly Jr. at 29 even though the Tunsil trade meant OT wasn't the most pressing need, passing on EDGE rushers the fanbase wanted. This is a GM who genuinely sticks to his board.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Very little mismatch between stated and actual philosophy. Peters walks the walk on BPA to a degree unusual for NFL GMs. The deviation is in his trade behavior: he claims to build through the draft but has traded significant draft capital for veterans (Tunsil, Lattimore, Samuel), depleting his 2025 and 2026 draft pick inventory. He may use free agency more aggressively to fill needs specifically so he can draft BPA.

trade_triggers: Player they had targeted specifically is available and they refuse to risk missing him (Conerly at 29 — turned down trade-back offers because they didn't want to risk losing him)
Acquiring veteran players who fill scheme needs mid-season (Lattimore, Samuel, Tunsil — all acquired via trade outside the draft)
Pick swap with division rival to acquire additional capital (traded down from 40 to 50 with Eagles in 2024, gaining an extra 2nd-round pick)
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Peters has been willing to spend future draft capital on veteran acquisitions. He traded 2025 and 2026 picks for Tunsil, and 2025 picks for Lattimore. He arrives at the 2026 draft with only 6 picks (2 in top 4 rounds). However, he consistently tries to recoup pick value in trades — the Tunsil deal included getting a 4th back, and the Lattimore deal included a 5th back. In-draft, he traded down in 2024 (40→50 with PHI, gaining pick 53). He is not a prolific trade-up GM.

position_avoidance: RB in early rounds — has not drafted a running back in any round across 2 drafts despite having need
IOL in early rounds — has addressed interior OL through free agency (Allegretti, Biadasz) rather than draft capital, though Brandon Coleman (Rd 3, 2024 OT/G) could count
position_premium: OT — Drafted OT in Round 1 (Conerly, 2025) and Round 3 (Coleman, 2024), plus traded heavily for Tunsil. OT is clearly among his top investment priorities.
DT — Took Jer'Zhan Newton at pick 36 despite having two starting DTs on premium contracts. Clear BPA conviction at this position.
CB — Invested in CB in Round 2 in both drafts (Sainristil 2024, Amos 2025), plus traded for Lattimore. Has spent more picks on CB than any other defensive position.

QB.archetype_summary: Dual-threat with elite rushing ability AND arm talent for downfield throws. Peters selected Jayden Daniels 2nd overall — a Heisman winner with record-breaking rushing ability for a QB, deep ball touch, and enough processing to run an NFL offense. Not a pure pocket passer, not a pure runner — a hybrid with elite mobility who can also throw with touch downfield.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Rushing ability and acceleration matter greatly. Daniels set the NFL record for most rushing yards by a rookie QB. Pocket mobility is prioritized alongside arm talent.

playing_style.primary_style: Mobile quarterback who can create outside the structure. Daniels' rushing ability was a primary attraction, not a bonus trait.
least_important_traits: Pure arm strength — adequate is fine if accuracy and ball placement are excellent

college_production.production_floor: Daniels was a Heisman Trophy winner with 40 TDs and only 2 INTs in his final season. Elite production was clearly valued.
college_production.production_trajectory: Daniels transferred from Arizona State to LSU and had a breakout Heisman season. Peters showed willingness to value a late-breakout trajectory at QB.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Daniels played in the SEC against top competition

examples: 2024 1.02 — Jayden Daniels, QB, LSU: 6-4, 210. Heisman winner. 40 TDs / 2 INT. 1,134 rushing yards. Deep ball touch, elite mobility. Won Offensive Rookie of the Year.

QB.confidence_note: Only 1 QB drafted. The Daniels pick was somewhat position-dictated by drafting 2nd overall with a massive need. Peters chose Daniels over Drake Maye and J.J. McCarthy — suggesting he valued the combination of dual-threat rushing and playmaking over pure pocket passing.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Peters has NOT prioritized EDGE in his two drafts despite clear need, suggesting extreme selectivity at the position. His only EDGE pick was a Day 3 developmental prospect. He may prefer to acquire EDGE via free agency/trade rather than draft. When he has drafted at the position, it's been a long, physical, run-defense-first type rather than a pure speed rusher.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-5+ preferred based on the one pick (Jean-Baptiste is 6-5, 250)
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: Insufficient data from WSH picks. 49ers tree suggests long arms valued.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a pure speed-first evaluation — Jean-Baptiste was described as 'not overly dynamic as an athlete'

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Based on 49ers lineage and Jean-Baptiste selection: length and run defense are prioritized. The 49ers under Lynch/Peters drafted EDGE players like Nick Bosa (elite all-around) and Arik Armstead (versatile, physical).
most_valued_traits: Scheme versatility (hand in dirt or standing up)
least_important_traits: Pure speed rush — has passed on speed-first EDGEs available in both drafts

college_production.production_floor: Jean-Baptiste had modest college production — not a volume sack producer. Peters may be more traits-focused at EDGE than production-focused.
college_production.production_trajectory: Jean-Baptiste had a late career spike. Peters may value ascending players.

examples: 2024 7.222 — Javontae Jean-Baptiste, EDGE, Notre Dame: 6-5, 250. 'Sets the edge in run defense,' described as not overly dynamic athletically but with room to grow technically. Day 3 developmental pick only.

EDGE.confidence_note: Only 1 EDGE drafted, in Round 7. Peters has conspicuously avoided drafting EDGE in early rounds despite glaring need in both 2024 and 2025. Preferred to address via free agency (Dorance Armstrong, Von Miller, Jacob Martin). The 2026 draft with pick 7 and massive EDGE need will be the real test of his EDGE archetype preference.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, fluid-moving tackle who excels in pass protection with zone-scheme versatility. Peters targets young tackles with elite foot quickness and movement skills over pure power and size. Both OTs drafted were athletic movers who can operate in zone-run schemes, not classic mauling road-graders. Tackle protection for Jayden Daniels is clearly a top organizational priority.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33+ inches (Conerly 33.5", Coleman played at similar range)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Foot quickness and lateral agility are prioritized. Conerly was described as 'the smoothest mover in drills at the combine.' Both were athletic tackles, not power-first.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Pass protection technique and foot quickness to protect the franchise QB. Zone-run ability as secondary emphasis.
least_important_traits: Pure power/anchor strength — both picks were noted as needing strength development, suggesting Peters values movement over anchor
deal_breakers: Stiff movers or tackles who lack lateral agility
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite foot quickness — Peters stayed at 29 and turned down trade-back offers specifically because he didn't want to miss Conerly

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starters. Conerly was a 2-year starter and All-American. Coleman was a multi-year starter at TCU.
college_production.production_trajectory: Both showed improvement from year to year. Ascending performance valued.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Peters specifically noted that Conerly's performance against Penn State's Abdul Carter (elite pass rusher) helped project his NFL readiness.

examples: 2025 1.29 — Josh Conerly Jr., OT, Oregon: 6-4.5, 311, 33.5" arms. 5.05 40, 34.5" vert. First-Team All-American, elite feet, zone-run fit. Age 21 at draft — extremely young.
2024 3.67 — Brandon Coleman, OT/G, TCU: 6-6, 320. Struggled in 2023 but graded 79.6 PFF in 2022. Athletic tackle with guard versatility.

IOL.archetype_summary: Peters has not drafted a traditional interior offensive lineman in two drafts. He has addressed IOL exclusively through free agency (Tyler Biadasz, Nick Allegretti, Andrew Wylie) and extensions (Sam Cosmi). Brandon Coleman was drafted as a tackle with guard flexibility, and may end up inside. This suggests Peters views IOL as a position to fill through FA rather than premium draft capital.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Based on free agency signings: versatility matters. Allegretti played center when Biadasz was injured.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Scheme versatility — ability to play multiple IOL positions

IOL.confidence_note: Zero IOL drafted. Entirely addressed through FA. May change if Coleman moves inside. Low priority for draft capital investment.

DT.archetype_summary: Disruptive, versatile interior defender with pass-rush ability and quickness. Peters took Jer'Zhan Newton at pick 36 — a smaller, quicker 3-technique who wins with hand usage, agility, and relentless motor rather than size and anchor. This was a clear BPA pick over need, as Washington already had Jonathan Allen and Daron Payne. Peters values interior disruption and pass-rush upside from the DT position.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Quickness and agility are the priority over size. Newton is undersized for a DT but exceptional in quickness.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.00 40 for a DT is notable (Newton ran ~4.95)

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Interior pass-rush disruption through quickness, hand usage, and leverage. Newton wins with active hands and impressive bend for a defensive tackle.
most_valued_traits: Versatility (can line up at multiple DL positions)
least_important_traits: Pure size/mass — willing to take undersized DTs with elite quickness
deal_breakers: One-dimensional run stuffers without pass-rush upside
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite interior pass-rush production — Newton was Big Ten DPOY and a Nagurski finalist

college_production.production_floor: Newton had 7.5 sacks in his final season and was a consensus All-American. Elite production was present.
college_production.production_trajectory: Newton was a 2-time first-team All-Big Ten and improved each year. Ascending production valued.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Peters noted Newton's production against top competition (Penn State game: 6 tackles, 7 pressures, 2 batted passes)

examples: 2024 2.36 — Jer'Zhan Newton, DT, Illinois: 6-2, 295-304, 32" arms. Big Ten DPOY, 7.5 sacks. Nagurski Trophy finalist. Disruptive 3-technique with elite quickness. Clear BPA pick — not a need. Had 5 sacks and 3 sacks in his first two NFL seasons.

CB.archetype_summary: Peters has drafted TWO different CB archetypes, suggesting scheme versatility matters more than a single physical template. In 2024, he took Sainristil — a small, ultra-athletic slot/nickel corner with WR conversion background and elite ball skills. In 2025, he took Amos — a long, physical press-man outside corner. The common thread is high football IQ, competitive toughness, and performance against top competition. The Quinn/Dan Quinn system demands man-coverage capability.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 to 6-1 — wide range spanning slot to outside
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard minimum — Sainristil at 30.88" (very short for CB) was drafted alongside Amos at 31.25". Length valued for outside corners but not a blanket requirement.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters (Sainristil 4.47, Amos 4.43 — both ran well). Explosiveness for Sainristil (40" vert, 94.5th percentile). For outside CBs, hip fluidity and press technique appear to matter more than raw speed.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.50 appears to be a soft floor

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Man coverage capability is non-negotiable given Quinn's scheme (Cover-1, Cover-3 press). Both drafted CBs have man-coverage skills.
least_important_traits: Pure zone-only corners unlikely to be drafted
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Peters specifically cited Amos' analytics profile: 'yards per coverage snap, not giving up a lot of explosives... five or six different metrics... a blue player, which is as high as you can get.'

college_production.production_floor: Sainristil: 6 INTs, All-American season (only 2 years at CB). Amos: 13 PBU, 3 INTs, First-Team All-SEC. Both had elite ball production in final seasons.
college_production.production_trajectory: Both were late movers to CB. Sainristil converted from WR. Amos transferred twice before his best season. Peters values ascending trajectories and isn't afraid of position converts.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high. Peters explicitly said 'performance against top opponents was weighted highly in the Commanders' scouting process.' Amos' grades against SEC receivers. Sainristil shadowed Marvin Harrison Jr.

examples: 2024 2.50 — Mike Sainristil, CB, Michigan: 5-10, 182, 30.88" arms. 4.47 40, 40" vert. WR convert. National champion. 6 INTs in 2023. 93 tackles and 14 PD as a rookie. Ultra-athletic, versatile slot/outside.

2025 2.61 — Trey Amos, CB, Ole Miss: 6-1, 195, 31.25" arms. 4.43 40. First-Team All-SEC. 13 PBU, 3 INTs. Long press-man corner. Physical at the LOS with length to disrupt. Described as fitting Dan Quinn's press-Cover-3 scheme perfectly.

S.archetype_summary: Big, rangy, developmental safety with length and speed. Peters took Dominique Hampton in Round 5 — a 6-2, 215-pound safety described as 'big, fast and long' who operates as a read-and-react high safety. This is a traits-over-production Day 3 pick fitting the 49ers mold of developing athletic DBs in later rounds (Talanoa Hufanga parallel).

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Size and length matter. Speed valued. Hampton's size was his calling card.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Single-high/read-and-react free safety type. Hampton was described as 'more comfortable operating as a read-and-react high safety than in man coverage.'
least_important_traits: Polished man coverage in the slot
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Physical tools — Peters/49ers tree has historically been willing to draft athletic safeties and develop them

college_production.production_floor: Hampton had 151 tackles and 11 PBU over his Washington career — steady but not spectacular production
college_production.production_trajectory: Late bloomer — didn't become full-time starter until 2022 (senior year)

examples: 2024 5.161 — Dominique Hampton, S, Washington: 6-2, 215. 6 seasons at UW. 151 career tackles, 11 PBU. Big, fast, long. Read-and-react high safety profile. Day 3 developmental/special teams pick.

S.confidence_note: Only 1 safety drafted (Day 3). Safety is a massive 2026 need (Quan Martin struggling, Will Harris limited). The 2026 draft at pick 7 may see Caleb Downs as a target, which would dramatically update this profile.

LB.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile linebacker with coverage upside. Peters took Jordan Magee in Round 5 — described as 'one of the most athletic linebacker prospects in his draft class.' This aligns with the 49ers tree (Fred Warner archetype: athletic LBs who can cover). Peters addressed LB primarily through FA (Bobby Wagner, Frankie Luvu — both All-Pro in 2024).

physical_profile.weight_range: Unknown — Magee profile suggests athletic, versatile build
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Athleticism is the premium. Magee was noted for elite athletic testing among LBs.

least_important_traits: Pure run-stuffing thumper profile not prioritized in draft — Wagner served that role via FA

examples: 2024 5.139 — Jordan Magee, LB, Temple: Round 5 athletic LB pick. Described as one of the most athletic LBs in the 2024 class. Developmental Day 3 selection.

LB.confidence_note: Only 1 LB drafted (Day 3). LB has been a FA-driven position (Wagner, Luvu). With Wagner likely retiring, LB becomes a 2026 need.

WR.archetype_summary: Quick, separating route runners with versatility rather than pure size/speed deep threats. Peters drafted Luke McCaffrey (Rd 3, 2024) — a slot-type WR with NFL bloodlines, foot quickness, and separation skills, and Jaylin Lane (Rd 4, 2025) — a speedy slot/return option with 4.34 speed. Both are undersized, quick-twitch separators, not big-bodied X receivers. Peters also traded for Deebo Samuel, suggesting he values versatile playmakers who can win in multiple ways.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed (Lane ran 4.34) and quickness. Foot quickness and route-running nuance over pure size.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.45 preferred based on small sample

playing_style.primary_role: Slot/versatile option rather than true X receiver
least_important_traits: Contested catch ability — neither drafted WR is a jump-ball winner

college_production.production_floor: McCaffrey had modest stats at Rice (smaller school). Lane had 69 catches/940 yards at MTSU. Neither was a volume producer at a Power 5 school, suggesting Peters values traits and projection over raw stats at WR.
college_production.production_trajectory: Both players transferred. Lane transferred from MTSU to Virginia Tech. McCaffrey transferred to Rice. Peters not deterred by transfers.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Peters likely adjusts for school/conference context — McCaffrey's Rice production in a non-Power 5 offense was not held against him.

examples: 2024 3.100 — Luke McCaffrey, WR, Rice: ~6-0, 200. NFL bloodlines (Christian McCaffrey's brother). Natural athlete, foot quickness, route running. Slot-type with separation ability.
2025 4.128 — Jaylin Lane, WR, Virginia Tech: 5-10, 196. 4.34 40. Transferred from MTSU. 2,532 career yards, 18 TDs. Semi-versatile slot with long speed and return ability.

WR.confidence_note: WR is a significant 2026 need with Deebo Samuel gone. Only drafted WRs in Day 2-3 so far. Peters may invest more heavily at WR in 2026.

TE.archetype_summary: Versatile, athletic hybrid TE/FB who can line up everywhere — inline, slot, backfield. Peters took Ben Sinnott in Round 2 — a former walk-on who was a TE/FB hybrid at Kansas State with elite combine testing (top vert for TEs, top 3-cone, top broad jump). The archetype mirrors the 49ers' George Kittle philosophy: find athletic TEs who can both block and receive, with versatility to move around the formation.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosiveness is key. Sinnott had a 40" vertical (top for TEs at combine), 10'6" broad, 6.82 3-cone (top for TEs). Athletic testing was a major driver of this pick.

playing_style.primary_role: Hybrid TE/FB — lines up inline, in slot, and in backfield as lead blocker
least_important_traits: Elite inline blocking technique — Sinnott's blocking was described as needing refinement
deal_breakers: Limited athletic upside — the combine testing was a major differentiator
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite combine performance at TE position

college_production.production_floor: 49 catches, 676 yards, 6 TDs in final season. 82 career catches, 1,138 yards, 10 TDs.
college_production.production_trajectory: Walk-on who developed into a 2nd-team All-American. Peters clearly values ascending development trajectories.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Run-heavy Kansas State offense — Sinnott's receiving production came in a context that depressed volume opportunities, which Peters likely credited

examples: 2024 2.53 — Ben Sinnott, TE, Kansas State: 6-4, 250, 4.68 40, 40" vert, 10'6" broad, 6.82 3-cone (all top marks for TEs). Walk-on who earned scholarship. 49 catches, 676 yards, 6 TDs in 2023. TE/FB hybrid who lined up at 4+ positions. PFF's 105th ranked player — seen as a reach by consensus but fit Peters' scheme-versatility preference.

RB.archetype_summary: Peters has NOT drafted a running back in two drafts and has addressed the position entirely through free agency (Austin Ekeler, Jeremy McNichols) or undrafted players. This is consistent with the 49ers tree philosophy of devaluing the RB position in the draft. Peters may break this pattern in 2026 with pick 7 given the strength of the RB class (Jeremiyah Love frequently mocked to WSH), but his baseline is to avoid spending premium draft capital on RB.
RB.confidence_note: Zero RBs drafted. The 49ers tree historically devalues RB in the draft (they traded for McCaffrey rather than drafting one high). Peters met with Jeremiyah Love at the 2026 combine, so a RB at pick 7 is not impossible, but it would be a departure from his established pattern.

reach_tolerance.notes: Peters' board appears to diverge significantly from media consensus boards. Newton at 36 was seen as a BPA pick (PFF had him as their 12th-ranked player). Sainristil at 50 was seen as a pick-swap value play. Sinnott at 53 was viewed by PFF as the 105th-ranked player — a significant perceived reach, but Peters' board clearly valued him much higher. Peters' front office builds its own board with custom analytics metrics and doesn't follow consensus. What looks like a 'reach' to the media may not be on Washington's board. Cooper DeJean (highly rated by media) was traded to a division rival, suggesting the Commanders rated him very differently.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Spread/multiple offense transitioning under new OC David Blough (promoted from assistant QB coach). Previously Kliff Kingsbury's spread RPO system. Blough is expected to install concepts similar to the Detroit Lions offense (where he studied). Zone-run concepts with RPO elements built around Jayden Daniels' dual-threat ability. Values versatile OL who can move in zone concepts, athletic TEs, and speed at WR.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: MAJOR SCHEME CHANGE FLAG: New DC Daronte Jones (from Minnesota Vikings) replacing Joe Whitt Jr. Jones will install 'multiple' defense — not a set 3-4 or 4-3, but different looks to confuse offenses. Dan Quinn confirmed Jones has full control and will install his own system, not Quinn's. Previously: Quinn's system was 4-3 base, Cover-1/Cover-3 heavy, among the highest man-coverage rates in the NFL. The new scheme may incorporate 3-4 elements and more disguised pressure packages.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: Significant defensive scheme change for 2026. Both OC (Blough replacing Kingsbury) and DC (Jones replacing Whitt) are new. Defensive draft targets should be evaluated for versatility and ability to play in multiple fronts. Quinn's historical preference for long, physical press-man corners and versatile DL remains relevant because he's still the HC, but Jones' scheme preferences may diverge. Jones' Vikings background suggests he may value versatile LBs and safeties who can disguise coverage. This creates uncertainty around defensive archetypes for the 2026 draft — apply a confidence discount of 0.15 to all defensive position archetypes.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate risk tolerance with an interesting split: Peters is conservative in the draft (sticking to his board, not reaching, taking high-floor players with scheme fit) but aggressive in trades for veterans (Tunsil, Lattimore, Samuel — spending future picks). In the draft, he's a board-disciplined, process-trusting GM who avoids panic picks. He drafted a walk-on TE in Round 2 and a WR-convert CB. He's willing to bet on non-traditional profiles if the evaluation supports it, which represents intellectual risk tolerance even if the picks themselves are high-floor.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate. After the 2024 NFC Championship loss where the offensive line struggled late-season, Peters' first pick in 2025 was an OT (Conerly). After the defense collapsed in 2025, Peters is expected to address defense heavily in 2026. However, his BPA discipline has historically prevented pure reactive drafting — he took Newton (DT) in 2024 even though the biggest loss the prior season was at QB, not DT.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Peters has shown willingness to cut bait on mistakes quickly. He traded Jahan Dotson (Ron Rivera-era 1st-round pick) to the Eagles for draft capital. He released Marshon Lattimore (his own trade acquisition) when the investment wasn't panning out. He released Tyler Biadasz (his own FA signing). This is a decisive, non-sentimental GM.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Balanced but analytics-informed. Peters uses a custom R&D/analytics department that generates proprietary metrics. For Sinnott, the elite combine performance (top vert, 3-cone, broad for TEs) likely boosted his stock. For Amos, Peters explicitly cited advanced analytics metrics ('yards per coverage snap, not giving up a lot of explosives... in their system a blue player, which is as high as you can get'). Peters uses testing data to validate film findings, with analytics as an additional confirmation layer. Film-first, but analytics can elevate a player from 'good' to 'our guy.'
psychological_profile.character_description: Moderate-to-high character emphasis with nuance. Peters stated character evaluations are 'case-by-case' and noted that the 49ers once drafted a player with reported character concerns who became 'one of the best teammates I've ever seen.' He's not rigidly anti-character risk but does extensive research through scouts at colleges and hometowns. Walk-on Ben Sinnott and team captain Mike Sainristil suggest Peters values high-character, high-work-ethic players.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Peters went all-in on QB with the 2nd overall pick. He selected Jayden Daniels and has built the roster around him. He is unlikely to draft another QB in the near future. The organizational priority is protecting Daniels (Tunsil trade, Conerly pick) and surrounding him with weapons. Peters views the franchise QB as the foundational piece and will allocate significant resources to support him.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Peters shows a balanced approach. Newton was the Big Ten DPOY with dominant production — clearly productive. But McCaffrey had modest numbers at Rice (smaller school), and Jean-Baptiste had limited college production. Peters seems to use production as a confirming signal rather than an eliminating threshold. At premium positions (QB, DT, CB), he's drafted highly productive players. At developmental Day 3 positions, he's willing to bet on traits with lower production. Tiebreaker appears to go to production, but Peters will draft a high-traits, lower-production player if the film and analytics validate the potential.

production_thresholds_by_position.QB: Heisman-level production (40 TDs/2 INTs for Daniels). Elite production required at QB.
production_thresholds_by_position.DT: 7+ sacks for a DT earmarked for early rounds (Newton had 7.5)
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: Ball production matters but context-adjusted. Sainristil had 6 INTs in only his 2nd year at CB. Amos had 13 PBU in 1 SEC season.
production_thresholds_by_position.OT: Multi-year starter minimum. All-conference caliber preferred.
production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No clear threshold — has drafted WRs with modest production from smaller schools
production_thresholds_by_position.TE: No clear threshold — Sinnott had 49 catches at a run-heavy school

college_production_preferences.notes: Peters explicitly values 'performance against top opponents.' He stated this was weighted highly in the evaluation of Trey Amos. He also cited Conerly's performance against Penn State's Abdul Carter. Context-adjusted production matters more than raw volume. The R&D department generates custom metrics that weight opponent quality.

CB.notes: Both CBs drafted ran sub-4.50 (Sainristil 4.47, Amos 4.43). Soft floor around 4.50.

DT.notes: Quickness/agility over size. Newton was 6-2, 295 — undersized. Peters clearly doesn't enforce a traditional DT size minimum.

OT.notes: Both OTs had 33+ inch arms. Conerly 33.5", Coleman similar. Consistent with league-wide OT thresholds.

TE.notes: Elite explosive testing valued. Sinnott's 40" vert, 10'6" broad, 6.82 3-cone were all top marks for TEs at the combine. Peters/49ers tree values athletic TEs.

WR.notes: Lane ran 4.34. Speed valued at WR. No hard height threshold — both WRs drafted were 5-10 to 6-0.

S.notes: Size valued. Hampton was 6-2, 215 — big for a safety. Consistent with 49ers tree (Hufanga was 6-0, 210).

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences rather than hard cutoffs. Peters' board is scheme-fit and analytics driven — a player with exceptional analytics/film metrics might override a measurable threshold. The exception may be OT arm length, where both picks met the 33" standard.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: Willing to go undersized (295) for a penetrating 3-tech. Does not require 300+ for interior DL.
size_preferences_by_position.CB: Wide range accepted — 5-10 slot to 6-1 outside. Role dictates size preference.
size_preferences_by_position.TE: 6-4, 250 — wants an athlete, not a blocking TE body type
size_preferences_by_position.WR: Undersized slot types (5-10 to 6-0) drafted so far. No big-bodied X receiver drafted.

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: The R&D department generates proprietary composite metrics. Peters cited Amos being 'in the 90th percentile in five or six different metrics.' Explosive testing (vertical, broad jump) appears important for TEs and safeties. Speed (40 time) matters for CBs and WRs. For DT, quickness/agility appears more important than raw speed.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first with analytics as a confirmation/elevation tool. Peters has drafted players whose combine testing was excellent (Sinnott, Sainristil) and players whose measurables were imperfect (Newton's short arms, Sainristil's small frame). When film and analytics align, Peters has conviction. When they diverge, film appears to win — but poor testing at a key metric (e.g., slow 40 for a CB) likely serves as an eliminator.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Highest-rated player on the board who fits the scheme. Peters has shown extreme discipline — in 2025 he turned down multiple trade-back offers at 29 because he had targeted Conerly specifically. Will not trade back if 'his guy' is on the board. Premium positions preferred (QB, OT in his history) but stated he doesn't have a 'premium positions' philosophy. Not afraid to take the unsexy pick if it's the right player.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Continues to draft BPA with scheme fit. Willing to trade within rounds for value (2024: swapped 40→50 with Eagles, gained extra pick at 53). This is where Peters begins to fill positional variety — in 2024, his three 2nd-round picks were DT, CB, TE, covering three different position groups. Day 2 is where Peters builds roster depth with players who should become starters.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, position versatility, and special teams contributors. Hampton (S, Rd 5) was a size/length projection. Magee (LB, Rd 5) was an athletic development pick. Jean-Baptiste (EDGE, Rd 7) was a run-defense specialist with growth potential. McCaffrey (WR, Rd 3 — end of Day 2) was a route-running technician. Peters uses Day 3 for traits-over-production developmental bets, consistent with the 49ers model of finding Kittle/Hufanga/Purdy-type later-round gems.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Limited data. Peters has not had comp picks in his WSH tenure. The 49ers under Lynch/Peters valued comp picks highly and would strategically let free agents leave to generate them.

QB.note: Jayden Daniels: Offensive Rookie of the Year 2024, franchise QB. Elite hit.

DT.note: Newton: 5 sacks in Year 2, emerging interior disruptor. Hit.

CB.note: Sainristil: 93 tackles, 14 PD as a rookie. Amos: 10 games as a starter. Both contributing.

OT.note: Conerly started 16 games as a rookie. Coleman has had limited impact so far.

TE.note: Sinnott: only 5 catches in rookie year. Slow development. Potential bust risk.

WR.note: McCaffrey and Lane have been limited contributors. McCaffrey did not start a game in 2025.

blind_spots: WR evaluation is a concern — 2 WRs drafted in middle rounds with neither becoming a significant contributor yet. The Deebo Samuel trade (which didn't pan out) and the WR room's ongoing weakness suggest Peters may struggle to evaluate receiving talent, though the sample is small and the picks were Day 3.
TE Sinnott was a Day 2 pick with limited production so far (5 catches in Year 1). May be too early to call but warrants monitoring.
strengths: QB evaluation: Daniels was an elite pick by any measure
CB evaluation: Both Day 2 CBs have been starters/contributors
DT evaluation: Newton taken as BPA at 36 despite having 2 DTs on roster — emerging as a quality player
OT evaluation: Conerly was an immediate starter as a 21-year-old

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Peters uses free agency aggressively to fill needs and create draft flexibility. In Year 1, he signed ~30 free agents to rebuild the roster from scratch. In Year 2, he signed cheap one-year veteran deals and made trades (Tunsil, Samuel, Lattimore). Peters has been criticized for the 2025 approach: signing aging veterans on short-term deals instead of young ascending FAs. For 2026, he has ~$82M in cap space and is expected to be much more aggressive in FA to fill defensive holes (EDGE, LB, S, CB), which would allow him to draft BPA at pick 7.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When Peters fills a position in FA, he still invests draft capital if BPA falls that way. He signed Zach Ertz at TE in 2024 but still drafted Sinnott at 53. He had DTs Jonathan Allen and Daron Payne but still drafted Newton at 36. Peters' stated and actual philosophy is to create 'optionality' through FA so he isn't forced into need-based drafting.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions, supplement through FA/trades. Peters stated he wants to 'build through the draft' but has shown willingness to spend significant trade capital for veteran upgrades when the price is right. He has expressed he wants to add 'youth, speed and talent' in 2026 after fielding the oldest roster in the NFL in 2025. Expects a shift toward younger, longer-term FA signings alongside the draft in 2026.

notable_patterns: Board discipline is extreme — Peters sticks to his board regardless of need. Took DT at 36 with 2 DTs on roster. Took OT at 29 after trading for Tunsil. Passed on EDGE in both drafts despite massive need.
Day 2 is his sweet spot for roster building — 5 of his 14 picks were in Rounds 2-3, and those have produced his best results (Newton, Sainristil, Sinnott, Coleman, Amos).
Has drafted 0 RBs, 0 IOL, and only 1 EDGE (Rd 7) in 2 drafts. These positions are filled through FA/trades.
CB is the most frequently addressed position in early rounds — drafted a CB in Round 2 in both drafts.
Prefers versatile, scheme-flexible players. Sainristil (WR→CB), Sinnott (TE/FB hybrid), Hampton (S with multiple position experience), McCaffrey (multiple roles).
Youth valued: Conerly was 21 at draft time, Newton was 21, Lane was young. Peters targets players with development runway.
Collaborative, analytics-integrated process. Custom R&D metrics influence board. Peters cited specific analytics percentiles for Amos in post-draft presser.

biggest_reaches.context: PFF's 105th-ranked player taken at 53. Significant reach by media consensus. Peters' board clearly valued him much higher based on combine performance and scheme fit as TE/FB hybrid.

biggest_steals.context: PFF's 12th-ranked player taken at pick 36. Considered an excellent value pick despite not filling a need. Big Ten DPOY.
Considered a steal by multiple draft graders. First-Team All-SEC outside CB with press-man skills.

2026_draft_context.missing_rounds: No 2nd-round pick (traded for Tunsil). No 4th-round pick (traded for Tunsil). Extremely limited Day 2 capital.
key_needs: EDGE — Massive need. Von Miller (37) is a FA. Dorance Armstrong missed most of 2025. No young EDGE talent on roster.
S — Major need. Quan Martin struggled badly in coverage. Will Harris limited. Need a transformational safety.
LB — Bobby Wagner (36) likely retiring. No established replacement.
WR — Need a No. 2 outside receiver next to McLaurin. Deebo Samuel gone. McCaffrey and Lane unproven.
RB — No established starting RB. Only Croskey-Merritt and Rodriguez under contract.
CB — Lattimore released ($18.5M cleared). Sainristil needs bounce-back. Amos looks promising but only 10 games of experience.
IOL — Center Biadasz released. Need interior starter.
2026_draft_context.likely_approach: Peters will attack free agency hard with ~$82M in cap space to fill EDGE, LB, S, and IOL needs, creating optionality to draft BPA at pick 7. Top candidates at 7 include EDGE (David Bailey, Rueben Bain Jr., Donovan Ezeiruaku), S (Caleb Downs), LB (Sonny Styles, Arvell Reese), or potentially RB (Jeremiyah Love). Peters' history suggests he will take the highest-rated player on his board regardless of position, even if it's an unexpected choice.

confidence_notes: 2 drafts as Commanders GM provide a meaningful baseline for behavioral patterns. The 49ers tenure (7 years as a key decision-maker) provides strong supplementary signal on evaluation philosophy. Key uncertainties: (1) Major defensive scheme change with new DC Daronte Jones creates uncertainty around defensive player archetypes; (2) Only 6 picks in 2026 draft means limited data will be generated; (3) Peters' aggressiveness in veteran trades may not persist — the 2025 season's regression may cause him to pull back on trading future picks; (4) Several drafted players are still early in their development, making hit-rate assessment preliminary.
